result:
The moon was once a seamstress. This is not a dream or the half-remembered line of a lullaby—the fact is baked into basalt and recorded in the lost library at Serephos, two dunes south of where the maps stop. Forget whatever Hollywood told you about gray dust and flagpoles. The ridges you see from your backyard are the pleats of an unfinished dress meant for the Earth. She measured us by tidal pulse, slipped pins into volcano mouths, cut continents from midnight silk. When the fit was finally perfect, she lifted a silver shears to release it—and stayed her hand. We were beautiful, yes, but we-shocked, warring, combustible. So she left the hem raw on the scissors and went into self-exile rather than hand us over. She still circles us, tailors’ eye evaluating, admiring, grieving.

There is a small café in Prague where coffee grounds arrange themselves into astronomical tables if the cup is turned three times counterclockwise by the right hand. The owner—elderly, hunchbacked like a question mark—claims to be the moon’s bookkeeper. He keeps records of every sigh that ever increased a tide. Meanwhile the craters keep count of every bullet we’ve fired upward since Galileo first gossiped about her scars. Moonlight, in this accounting, is an interest payment on an unpaid debt. The old man charges one overlooked memory per espresso. Locals walk out lighter by several seconds from childhood; tourists leave missing phone numbers they never intended to dial.

I forgive quicker in moonlight, but I also burn letters quicker there. Last night I held eleven unsent envelopes above a tin pail on my balcony, struck matches that looked like tiny comets dying. The pages curled and said my name as if to warn me. I thought about how paper and flesh both contain cellulose, wondered whether smoke climbs in sympathy because it remembers trees. I listened for the sound of shears shutting again across the vacuum.

My upstairs neighbor, Rose, believes the moon is a conch shell pressed to God’s ear. She plays cello in the stairwell at 2:17 a.m. because overtones locate spiritual cavities. Once, after a botched sonata, she knocked on my door smelling like resin and thunderstorm. She said she had started bleeding lunar phases; the crescents under her fingernails waxed blue. I offered gauze but she lifted her palms as if holding a baby bird made of impossible light. It weighed nothing, which made me ache more than grief.

Sometimes I meet old versions of myself in thrift stores—someone wearing my ninth-grade loneliness in the form of an oversized T-shirt, another carrying my thirties anxiety home in a chipped ceramic mug priced 45¢. I never speak. I simply watch to see if they treat the artifacts kindly. Once a boy of maybe twenty bought the Polaroid I took of the Atlantic at dusk: three abandoned sandcastles, one gull crying into a barcode of dying light. The paper had buckled in my top drawer for a decade. He paid cash, bounced on his heels, said the picture reminded him of his mother’s voice the day she taught him the word *infinite*. How could I tell him it was just one evening when everything felt collapsible? Instead I nodded, and when he left the bell above the door jingled like a snapped leash.

I think cities are organisms that molt steel skins every century. Beneath new metros lie fossils of older rails, remember how whistles once praised the curvature of the earth. Workers discover them when they blast into bedrock and find rails ribbed like whale bones. The metal smells of coal and spearmint, inexplicably. They carry pieces home, prop them above fireplaces. Children ask for stories, so parents invent train-gods whose wheels spoke constellations, saying the first maps were lullabies sung by locomotives. None of it is true, and all of it fits.

This afternoon at the laundromat I watched a woman fold galaxies. She lifted dryersheets embossed with star charts, shook them so nebulae unfolded like laundry. She told me recession velocities can be reduced with lavender softener. She claimed her socks, paired correctly, sang in wavelengths audible to moths. When I laughed, her eyes flashed quasar-dangerous. “Entropy is just shrinkage,” she said, “and all of us are trying to keep our love from winding up doll-sized.” Then she offered me one warm towel smelling of cosmic microwave background and told me to wrap it over my eyes. I saw, in the redwash behind lids, the first alphabet—thirteen gestures between hydrogen atoms deciding whether to keep clapping or scatter into silence.

At dinner I cut an onion and found the moon inside it. Pearly, exact, translucent. Its rings spoke the same equations Rose had drawn on her wrist with eyeliner: rules for translating sorrow into tide. I cried, not out of irritation but from reunion. Salt to salt, water to water, onions to moons—the transaction unremarked by the knife. I chopped faster so the circles wouldn’t notice their dismembering. Midway I recognized the pattern: the rings grew tighter toward the heart, miniature orbits. Somewhere the seamstress watches, maybe, deciding if we finally deserve sleeves.

There is a legend—one of those living stories not yet committed to book—that says if every person on the night side of Earth hums the same pitch at exactly midnight UTC, the moon will sigh in dropped-stitch relief. The sound, traveling through vacuum by shameless metaphor, will quilt the sky ten percent kinder for one lunar month. Children half a world away will awaken with clearer dreams; stray dogs will walk into shelters on their own, remembering language. The problem is no one agrees on which pitch. Some say 432 hertz, others 528, still others insist on B-flat fifty-seven octaves below middle-C based on black-hole recordings. The dissonance keeps tectonic plates shifting, keeps spouses arguing over the toothpaste cap. We are so close.

Tonight I tuned three humming apps, layered sine waves against one another until I found the interval my blood makes when oxygen leans in shy. Rose knocked. She had brought her cello case, lunar frost whitening the edges of her hair. Together we climbed the fire escape to the roof. The city folded its lights inward like an embarrassed fan. 11:59:57… We breathed. In each of our chests, a small ocean rocked against clavicles. We hummed—not perfectly, but with conviction. The note was neither major nor minor; it belonged to apple cores gone soft in wooden drawers, to train whistles learned by grasshoppers, to the seamstress pausing mid-cut. It lasted fourteen heartbeats longer than a minute.

For an instant the moon flashed ultraviolet, an apology in a color our eyes are still learning to receive. Probably it was the retinas adjusting to darkness. Probably the sound bounced off low cloud and simply returned larger. All I know is the wind got silkier, smelled faintly of hot photocopier, of absolution. When I looked at Rose she had unbuttoned her sleeves, rolled fabric to the elbow. Beneath the skin, faint silver threads glistened, half-hooked, waiting. She smiled the way a planet must when neighboring eclipses agree to share orbit.

We didn’t build utopia that night. Buses still ran late. Someone, somewhere still ate loneliness for dinner. But the edge softened enough that at 3:12 a.m. a man checking his mailbox found a letter he’d written to himself at seven, containing only one sentence: *It’s not your job to finish the world.* He read it twice, laughed rain, went indoors. He made tea. In the swirl of milk he recognized the same spiral visible in fingerprints, hurricane cores, onion middles, lunar maria. He added honey, not to fix anything but to keep the pattern company.

Later, when insomnia returned his keys, I held up the ruined scissors from the seamstress’s abandoned kit. They reflect moonlight in transverse thirds now—partial memory, partial prophesy. I set them on the windowsill like one sets a bowl of water for ghosts. Their crossed blades form the letter X, which is how kisses looked before alphabets. Rose says tomorrow she will tune her cello to that note we found, try to hear underneath Detroit thunder for where the dress might still be hiding. She asked if I wanted to help look. I told her yes, but only if we bring the onion remains. Salt to salt, ring to orbit, sigh to tide. We might not retrieve the whole gown, but salvaging even one continent shaped like a cuff or a pocket would be start.

The moon doesn’t speak in dreams anymore; she hangs in the window like thread in a cat’s mouth—loose, inevitable, alive with possibilities.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
